data.title: Marvell: Load Up Now While The Market Runs For Cover
data.description:
Marvell: Disappointing Outlook Shows That The Market Was Right After All

Wow! Down almost 20% to end Friday's trading after what was arguably a disappointing guide on the forward outlook of Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL). With yesterday's waterfall decline, investors are given a snapshot of what a possible market reckoning could look like if growth-related tech companies don't live up to the market's prior high expectations linked to the AI infrastructure prospects.

No surprises, as MRVL suffered one of its worst plunges since early April. In addition, I think it's noteworthy to consider that the stock has failed to regain the highs that it delivered in early 2025, even as semiconductor peers (SMH) have gone on to establish new peaks in August. I think it's critical to consider that the opportunity underpinning Marvell's premium valuation has always been predicated on its ability to outperform the market's growth cadence.

Yet, what we got from management at Marvell's fiscal second quarter earnings call was cautious commentary about possible delayed revenue ramp from custom chips, potentially linked to delayed buildout with Microsoft (MSFT). Note that I had already cautioned that Marvell's near-term growth metrics could have peaked in Q1, so the circumspection shouldn't be considered as an anomaly.

I think it only goes to show that the market has gotten their narrative on MRVL on point, as the stock has continued to hover well below the 2025 zenith. If you want the market to accord you a substantive growth premium, then you must validate that in your performance and guidance, as the market has little patience for anything less than pristine execution. Otherwise, we must accept the market's "punishment" and valuation de-rating is apt, giving the company more time to regain the bullish proposition.

Let me clear about what I think about the expanding AI growth opportunity for the leading semiconductor companies. I think it's pretty obvious that Marvell is still very well positioned, given its diversified exposure into the data center custom chips, networking, and also the non-AI space (including automotive, industrial, carrier, and enterprise).

Yet, there's also no doubt that AI is being positioned as the main growth pillar behind Marvell's data center segment, which accounted for more than 74% of its overall Q2 revenue base. In essence, anything that could throw a spanner into the works suggesting that we could face a delayed growth inflection toward the second fiscal half of Marvell's revenue ramp, could still cause the market to reassess their conviction.

Marvell: Margins Continue To Perform Well

Still, I think the market believes that unless the hyperscalers (in particular Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft) step up their efforts significantly into their custom chips projects, we should expect Marvell's growth to continue normalizing through the next 12 months.

And for that matter, I think we must also contemplate increased competitive risks from Broadcom (AVGO), as the semiconductor bellwether flexes its dominance in the custom chips and AI networking segment. With Nvidia (NVDA) also seeking to expand its growth horizon beyond just purveying the most cutting edge merchant AI chips and deeper into the entire AI infra stack, we could see more intense challenges against Marvell's growth prospects.

Yet, I'm also pleased to note that management has assuaged investors that we shouldn't see a marked moderation in Marvell's margins. In contrast, MRVL's adjusted operating margin reached 34.8% in the second fiscal quarter, corroborating the bullish thesis of delivering higher margin custom chips and optical DSPs that are now pivotal to Marvell's medium-term margins outlook.

Therefore, even though the cyclical weakness in its non-data center business has affected the overall growth momentum, I believe the ongoing multi-year buildout of the AI infra theme should help Marvell navigate these near-term challenges.

Buy Or Sell MRVL Stock?

While yesterday's selling fervor arguably took out most of MRVL's June gains, it hasn't completely eviscerated buying support aligned with what is still a long-term uptrend.

As seen in the above chart, MRVL's robust buying momentum at the critical support zones along its 50-month moving average (blue line) should provide credence for investors thinking about catching the falling knife following its Q2 earnings scorecard.

And as I gleaned MRVL's forward EBITDA multiple of 16.7x coming in well below its 10Y average of 20x, I believe the market has yet again taken their pessimism too far against a well-established leader in the custom chips and AI networking space.

In other words, has investors accorded too much doubt to the company's ability to ramp up in the second fiscal hard, as these conceivably "lumpy" custom ASIC growth projects with the hyperscalers take more time to pan out?

While I acknowledge that such execution risks cannot be downplayed, I believe Marvell's top customers remain determined to build out their capabilities in custom AI chips. And Marvell's performance shows that they are keen to diversify beyond Broadcom (in ASIC) and Nvidia (in merchant AI chips), affording them an opportunity to strengthen the resilience in their supply chain. Nevertheless, I also understand that a delayed infra ramp up could also be complicated by a slowdown in CapEx uplift toward the second half of CY2026, which might also impede Marvell's prospects through the current fiscal year.

Yet, while these headwinds could hinder a more robust valuation upgrade, I think the current skepticism in its ability to execute the recovery is overstated.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: What Investors Should Expect From The August 2025 Job Report
data.description:
On Friday, September 5, 2025, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will publish the employment situation for August. Investors will likely expect disappointment in the upcoming figures. In addition, the BLS might post downward revisions for prior months.

July 2025 Employment Data and Revisions

In July, nonfarm payroll employment increased by 73,000. This has changed a little since April. The unemployment rate of 4.2% is consistently in a trend that started last year in May.

More importantly, the BLS issued a larger revision than normal for May and June employment. In May, it lowered the figure by 125,000. This brought May employment to +19,000. In June, the employment count was revised by 133,000, from 147,000 to 14,000. The two revisions lowered May and June employment by 258,000.

The BLS explained that “Monthly revisions result from additional reports received from businesses and government agencies since the last published estimates and from the recalculation of seasonal factors.” Instead of monthly NFP changes in the 100,000 to 150,000 range (highlighted in blue), May and June jobs diminished significantly.

The report drew a reaction from U.S. President Trump. He fired BLS Commissioner Erika McEntarfer. At the time, he wrote that “someone more competent and qualified” would replace the Commissioner. Weeks later, he nominated E.J. Antoni, the chief economist at the conservative Heritage Foundation, as the next commissioner.

Expected August Job Growth

The health care sector likely added more jobs in August, just as it did in July (+55,000). It will likely exceed the average monthly gain of 42,000 over 12 months. Jobs increased in hospitals, nursing care facilities, the offices of physicians, dentists, and other health practitioners.

Investors may bet that Medicaid plan providers will continue to rebound. In the last month, UnitedHealth (UNH), Elevance (ELV), Humana (HUM), and Centene (CNC) all gained at least 10% or more.

On Aug. 22, a U.S. judge paused changes to the Affordable Care Act marketplace. More than 2.2 million people could have lost healthcare coverage. Even after the rally, the valuation grades for the firms range from B to an A+.

UnitedHealth is especially compelling. Several firms, including Renaissance Technologies, disclosed investments in the company. Readers who prefer to pick companies with the highest industry rank might consider Humana instead. This company is ranked third. HealthEquity (HQY) (not shown) is ranked first on this Quant filter.

In August, jobs in social assistance might increase. Jobs increased by 18,000, led by a 21,000 increase in individual and family services.

Unchanged Monthly Labor Levels

The BLS report highlighted that jobs did not change by much in July for mining, quarrying, and oil and gas extraction. In August, expect companies in those sectors to take a wait-and-see approach to hiring. At the start of the month, tariff rules on copper imports settled at 50%. The government hiked tariffs on steel and aluminum on Aug. 19.

On August 29, the U.S. appeals court ruled that most Trump tariffs are not legal. This reignites tariff-related uncertainties. It might cause companies to continue pausing their plans to hire staff.

Job Cuts in Government

Job losses in the Federal Government might fall again in August. In July, the government cut 12,000 jobs. The decline will continue in future monthly NFP reports. The BLS wrote that “employees on paid leave or receiving ongoing severance pay are counted as employed in the establishment survey.” When those payments end, government job cuts will increase.

Even though government jobs are falling, investors should continue to hold companies that benefit from government spending. After social security, net interest, Medicare, and health spending, national defense is the fifth-biggest item in dollars for FY 2025.

The Golden Dome project is a positive catalyst. Considerations from the Trump administration to take equity stakes in defense contractors like Lockheed Martin (LMT) are a catalyst for the military sector. In the last month, shares of RTX (RTX), General Dynamics (GD), Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman (NOC), and L3Harris (LHX) rose. TransDigm Group (TDG) lost 12.93%. 8/29/2025

TDG stock fell as much as 13% after it posted fiscal third-quarter results. Adjusted earnings per share of $9.60 did not meet analyst expectations. The commercial original equipment manufacturing segment did not perform well. CEO Kevin Stein said that “Sales in the commercial OEM market fell short of our expectations, primarily due to lower than anticipated OEM build rates and inventory destocking.”

Among the defense contractors, shares of TDG and RTX score a D+ on valuation. However, all of the firms mentioned score an A or higher on profitability.

Your Takeaway

Data from the nonfarm payroll report in the last three months is highly volatile. Organizations are likely reacting to the daily developments in U.S. tariff policies. The trade deals in late July, such as the U.S./EU trade deal and the Asian deal before that, should help corporations. They will expect a tariff rate range. As a result, they may make hiring decisions. That would help strengthen future job market reports.

When the Federal Reserve meets, it will more likely cut interest rates by 25 bps. The August job report will not indicate a strong enough shift to dissuade the bank from that decision.

data.title: Green Plains: Transforming Ethanol Production From Zero To Hero
data.description:
Thesis

Green Plains (NASDAQ:GPRE) is an ethanol producer that is in the process of transitioning its business from operating at a consistent cash burn to one that is free cash flow positive. Through operational improvements and the realization of additional income from the startup of the Trailblazer pipeline.

This pipeline is connected to three of the company’s ethanol plants in Nebraska. Green Plains has installed the necessary equipment to begin carbon capture at these facilities for eventual sequestration. The company plans to monetize the associated carbon credits that are afforded by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act to boost revenues and EBITDA. Initial estimates expect this project to develop between $100-$150 million in EBITDA, making this a transformative project for Green Plains.

In addition to the Trailblazer pipeline, GPRE has additional room for growth through a similar project with Summit Carbon. This proposed project would give the company a second leg to its decarbonization strategy and is expected to be online in Q1 of 2027.

The company remains cheaply valued based on 2026 EBITDA expectations with further upside provided by operational improvements, debt reduction, and more aggressive adoption of E15 fuels. These factors are deserving of a BUY rating.

Who Is Green Plains?

GPRE produces ethanol for nine plants located in the mid-west for a combined capacity of 780 million galls per year. The company has recently been through a restructuring that resulted in a CEO change as well as multiple asset divestitures.

The divestitures have significantly improved the operational focus of the company while also strengthening the balance sheet. The company is focusing on improving the cash generation abilities of the base ethanol business while simultaneously paying down over $150 million in debt since March.

As a stand-alone business, the ethanol plants have not done well over the last several years. The challenging biofuels market has caused the company to be generally in cash burn mode. To address this, the company has reduced its costs by $50 million through both OPEX and G&A efficiencies.

More recently, the company also announced it had sold its Obion plant for $190 million. From the map above you can see that this plant was a geographic outlier from the rest of the company’s operating footprint and likely did not fit into the company’s long-term carbon capture strategy (more on that later).

Additive to the asset sale, the proceeds are being allocated to repaying junior secured mezzanine notes that carried an interest rate of 11.75%. Paying back this loan clearly provides a financial uplift to the company’s bottom line but also removes covenants against four of the company’s remaining ethanol plants.

Carbon Capture Is The Main Story Line

As I alluded to in my main thesis, GPRE expects significant EBITDA gains from carbon capture. The company has entered into a transportation agreement with Tallgrass LLC to utilize the Trailblazer pipeline for carbon sequestration in Wyoming. This pipeline was formerly used as a natural gas pipeline but has since been converted for CO2 applications.

GRPE has outfitted its three ethanol plants in Nebraska with carbon capture technology to feed into this pipeline and to harvest both 45Q and 45Z tax credits. Initially, only the 45Q tax credits are expected to meet the $100-$150 million in EBITDA goals set for the project. This will be done through a basic strategy of selling off the generated carbon credits.

To keep this discussion simple, I will just point to how the company’s former CEO described the process in the Q3-2024 conference call.

So, monetizing the 45Z and monetizing the carbon credit will be something that there are well-developed markets to do that. We've seen those trades in that 90% 95% of face value happen and that's in our numbers as well. So, we're going to sell those credits and monetize them and not use them internally unless we need to down the road from a standpoint of then we can realize 100% monetization of those credits. So, we add all that revenue up, we discounted by somewhere between 5% and 10% to get to our net revenue, deduct our transport fees, a little bit of operational cost for the facility to get to an EBITDA number.

Further down the development pipeline, the company plans to use the enhanced carbon footprint of its ethanol plants to become eligible in the production of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF). Should this venture be successful, the company would then have exposure to a new high margin product. Since ethanol is generally a low-margin business, this avenue may provide an EBITDA uplift to the company’s baseline ethanol business.

In addition to Trailblazer, GPRE has also committed four additional plants to a prospective sequestration project through Summit Carbon Solutions. In a similar arrangement to Trailblazer, the company would provide captured carbon to harvest additional 45Q credits. This project is still in the permitting phase, but the company expects this project to be in service by the 1st quarter of 2027.

Valuation

Green Plains has historically produced volatile EBITDA. On average, the firm has produced at roughly an operational breakeven. Negative cash flows have thus resulted from CAPEX and interest expenses.

On a trailing basis, the firm trades at over 30x EBITDA. However, following a $50 million cost reduction effort, the company appears capable of generating an annual EBITDA of approximately $50 million from the base business. Adding the $125 million benefit from the Trailblazer sequestration project drives YE 2026 to approximately $175 million. On a forward basis, the company currently trades at roughly 6x 2026 EBITDA, implying a solid value play once this project is fully operational.

The sale of the Obion plant provides another layer of value for shareholders. Prior to the announcement, GPRE’s enterprise value was worth $1 billion. However, after reporting a $190 million sale on the plant, the company’s new market cap sits at approximately $940 million, or around a 5-6% discount.

This is after the 11% gain in the stock price earlier this week. This implies that the company is trading a lower EV:EBITDA multiple after the transaction than before it. In simple terms, even though the stock price has risen substantially, the market is not accurately pricing in the effect of this transaction. This becomes even more true when considering the notes that were retired carried nearly a 12% interest rate.

Risks

The risks associated with GPRE are clearly centered around the company’s reliance on government regulation. Not only are revenues subject to risk with changing administrations over time, but also the speed at which the government operates.

The federal government has never been mistaken as being a smooth operating machine. As a result, even if the 45Q/45K tax credits are here to stay, they need to be physically awarded for GPRE to sell them. Regulatory and government delays can present a significant hurdle to realizing the projected cash flows for either of the company’s CCS projects.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. In its current form GPRE is a company that has historically generated negative free cash flow.
  2. The company has driven $50 million in operating costs out of the business as part of its restructuring plan.
  3. Sale of the Obion plant significantly de-levers the company while also removing high-cost debt.
  4. Carbon sequestration is expected to provide a significant uplift in EBITDA for 2026. The company currently trades at 6x 2026 EBITDA implying solid future value.
  5. Based on the difference in pre-and-post transaction enterprise value, I do not believe the market has adequately priced in the value of the Obion sale.
  6. Reliance on government entities presents regulatory risks to revenue collection.

data.title: Peak Humans: The Megatrend No One Is Talking About (Yet)
data.description:
We are on the cusp of a major and unprecedented turning point in human history.

For the first time in as long as humans have roamed the earth, the global human population is about to peak and begin an indefinite decline.

Of course, there have been temporary dips in the human population due to plagues, wars, and natural disasters, but what is coming will be entirely different. The depopulation to come will be entirely of humanity's own choosing, and it will be indefinite rather than transient.

Well before the end of this century, as early as the 2050s and as late as the 2080s, the worldwide population of human beings will start shrinking.

Demographic facts make this inevitable.

Fertility rates in the developed world have been in a steady decline since women began entering the workforce en masse in the 1960s, and birth rates renewed their downtrend in the wake of the Great Financial Crisis of 2008-2010.

For a variety of reasons, people are having fewer and fewer babies. This is a worldwide phenomenon. No corner of Planet Earth is spared from it.

In about 30 countries around the world, including Japan, South Korea, China, and parts of Europe, populations are already shrinking. Many other countries, including the United States, have entered a period of sharply decelerating population growth. Most of these are only maintaining any population growth at all because of net immigration.

At this point, even India, the largest country in the world by population, has fallen below the technical "replacement rate" of 2.1 children per adult woman. Indonesia is right on the cusp of it.

Only Africa, particularly sub-Saharan Africa, maintains a high fertility rate. But across Africa, the same downtrend in fertility holds true. The continent is simply a half-century or so behind the rest of the world.

Right now, in 2025, the global fertility rate is estimated to be 2.2, just barely above the replacement rate. If trends continue, then humanity will fall below the replacement rate in the near future.

A few other scattered outliers like Afghanistan, Pakistan, Kazakhstan, and Uzbekistan also stand out for their relatively high fertility rates.

Two-thirds of humans live in countries with fertility rates below the replacement rate.

The latest projection from the United Nations foresees the global population peaking at over 10 billion in the mid-2080s, but at a mere 0.5 births-per-woman less than their baseline assumption, the forecasted peak population moves forward to the mid-2050s at less than 9 billion people.

So far, in the few years since this forecast came out, the world is trending more toward that low-fertility scenario. That, to my mind, makes it more likely that the global population will peak in the 2050s or 2060s -- three or four decades from now.

I will be in my 60s or 70s during that period, which means I have a pretty good shot at living to see Peak Humans.

One might think that the peak and subsequent decline of the human population is a long way off and thus future generations' problem. That couldn't be further from the truth. The consequences of Peak Humans are already being felt, and they will only increase over time.

In what follows, I want to sketch out some preliminary thoughts on the economic impact and investment implications of decelerating population growth and eventually outright population decline.

It isn't a mere hypothetical. It's a current reality in many parts of the world and demographic destiny in the rest of it.

Afterward, as usual, I will cover my dividend growth stock buy list and make some brief comments about a few of my picks.

From Baby Boom To Baby Bust

The 20th century witnessed an explosion in the global human population. In 1925, humanity numbered about 2 billion. By 2025, our numbers had swelled to over 8 billion.

But the baby boom of the 20th century is rapidly shifting into a baby bust in the 21st.

In 2012, global births peaked at a little over 146 million and began to decline thereafter.

In 2008, the population of Japan peaked, and it has been steadily declining ever since.

Portugal's population peaked in 2009. Greece's, in 2010. Italy's, in 2017. South Korea's, in 2020. And China's, in 2021.

The most recent projection from the Congressional Budget Office forecasts that, assuming moderate and stable net immigration, the US population will continue to grow through the mid-2050s. But in a zero-immigration scenario, the US population would peak and begin to decline in 2033.

While the CBO always projects an unrealistic constant far into the future, the immediate reality of immigration is very different than the above chart shows.

According to recent data from Pew Research, net immigration has turned negative this year for the first time in half a century. To wit:

After more than 50 years of rapid growth, the nation’s immigrant population is now in decline.

In January 2025, 53.3 million immigrants lived in the United States – the largest number ever recorded. In the ensuing months, however, more immigrants left the country or were deported than arrived. By June, the country’s foreign-born population had shrunk by more than a million people, marking its first decline since the 1960s.

A July report put out by the American Enterprise Institute estimates that net immigration this year is likely to be -200,000 people, give or take a few hundred thousand. Next year, net immigration is likely to be even more deeply in the negative.

If this situation persists, then the peak US population will be brought forward from the 2050s to the 2030s, rapidly hastening the US's entry into the club of shrinking countries.

What if there is a rebound in fertility rates? Some might insist that child tax credits should have some effect on the birth rate.

Unfortunately, though, from South Korea to the Scandinavian countries, no pro-natalist policy that has ever been tried anywhere in the world has significantly and sustainably increased the fertility rate (see, for example, here). Typically, there is a very small and temporary bump in fertility as couples who wanted kids anyway accelerate their family planning, followed by a return to the prior downtrend.

There are many factors behind this downtrend in fertility rates, and most of them cannot be plausibly reversed.

In a recent paper considering why fertility continues to fall in high-income countries, economists Melissa Kearney and Phillip Levine argue that "the evidence points to a broad reordering of adult priorities with parenthood occupying a diminished role."

In their book After the Spike: Population, Progress, and the Case for People, economists Dean Spears and Michael Geruso argue that the opportunity cost of parenting is higher today than it ever has been.

Here are some quotes from pages 224-225:

Parenting a child, or another child, could mean scaling back educational goals, career plans, or other ambitions.

... In decades past, there was less to lose by spending time on children. The opportunity costs of parenting today include vacations, restaurant meals, quality time with a partner, streaming any good song or movie ever made, and just hanging out while the dishwasher and laundry machines do their things.

... A better world, with better options, makes parenting worse by comparison.

Combining these two explanations, then, we might conclude that couples of childbearing age increasingly prioritize other things besides having children, whether that's careers or Mediterranean vacations or quiet evenings to binge Game of Thrones.

No amount of child tax credits, free childcare, or other pro-natalist benefits will change that.

The Economic & Investment Consequences

As the quote attributed to Yogi Berra goes, "It's hard to make predictions, especially about the future."

It is especially hard to make predictions about something to come in the future has never happened before in human history.

Nevertheless, I have some educated guesses about the economic and investment implications of the baby bust.

The most foundational point is that the economy is made up of people. That is, it is basically the aggregation of human activity, from production to consumption to innovation and investment.

All else being equal, more humans translates into more of all of those things: more production, more consumption, more innovation, and more investment.

With fewer humans, you get a smaller labor force, less aggregate consumption, less innovation, and less investment.

Consumption

As the population of humans peaks and then declines, aggregate consumption decelerates, then stagnates, then finally begins to shrink.

Consumer staples (XLP) and consumer discretionary (XLY) companies basically have two ways to organically increase sales: unit volume growth and premiumization (selling higher-cost, higher-margin products).

After a country's population peaks, consumer goods companies can continue to grow through premiumization, but competition for a shrinking pool of consumers makes volumetric growth much harder.

In Japan, for example, inflation-adjusted consumption did not peak until Q1 2014, over five years after the country's population peaked.

Since 2014, however, Japan's real consumption has been stagnant, at best.

To put it crudely, fewer humans means fewer mouths to feed, bodies to clothe and shelter, and brains to entertain with toys, games, trinkets, books, and so on.

Of course, consumer goods companies can go outside their national borders in search of customers, but what happens when populations across the developed world begin to decline?

With this in mind, I tend to think that the glory days of consumer giants like Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), and Procter & Gamble (PG) are slowly fading. For now, there is still volumetric growth to be found in the US and certain other parts of the world, but the closer we get to Peak Humans, the harder revenue growth will become.

Production/Labor Markets

As population growth slows, especially among the working-age group, the amount of job growth needed to maintain a steady unemployment rate declines.

So, all else being equal, we should expect to see job growth slow as population growth slows and shrink once the population begins to shrink.

Unfortunately, in the US, there is a growing labor market mismatch. The last few generations of American high schoolers have been strongly encouraged to go to college rather than trade schools or blue-collar apprenticeships.

This is had led to a situation wherein the population of working-age, non-college educated, native-born Americans has been shrinking for the past decade and a half.

And now, as artificial intelligence tools replace much of the entry-level work that recent college graduates would do, we appear to have a mild oversupply of college-educated workers in some fields.

Meanwhile, in countless blue-collar and manual labor industries, there is a persistent labor shortage. Baby Boomers, who weren't pushed toward college to nearly the same degree as Millennials and Gen Zers, are retiring from these blue-collar industries and leaving job openings that there simply aren't enough willing and qualified workers to fill.

The surge of low-skilled immigrants in the post-COVID years helped to fill many of those job openings, but now the net immigration tides are going out. This will inevitably stunt the labor market and economic growth.

To quote the aforementioned American Enterprise Institute report:

Slowing immigration will put significant downward pressure on growth in the labor force and employment. Potential employment growth, meaning employment growth when the labor market is operating sustainably at “full employment,” could be between 10,000 and 40,000 jobs a month in the second half of 2025 (down from 140,000 to 180,000 in 2024), and potential job growth could turn negative in the second half of Trump’s term.

Unless unemployed white-collar workers begin to take jobs on construction sites, manufacturing floors, and auto repair shops, this GDP-stunting labor market mismatch looks likely to persist for a long time to come.

Japan, China, and Europe have experienced similar labor market mismatches. The richer a country gets, the less its people want to do physically demanding jobs and the more they want to do knowledge-based work in air conditioned buildings.

In the long run, however, stagnating aggregate consumption will cause labor demand to similarly stagnate.

Innovation And Investment

Finally, we get to innovation and investment, which is perhaps the most important part of the story.

A common strain of thought is that AI and automation will solve all the economic problems of depopulation and create a techno-utopia for the ever-shrinking population of humans.

There are two counterarguments to this hypothesis.

First, innovation comes from people. Therefore, the fewer people there are, the less innovation there is likely to be.

To quote Spears and Geruso from After the Spike again:

Because people create ideas, fewer useful ideas will be created in a depopulating future than on a stabilized path.

... Without people to do the discovering, innovating, and testing, less creation will happen. Less advancement. Less progress.

Inventors and luminaries are rare. With fewer people, there are likely to be fewer inventors and luminaries as well.

Second, larger populations offer greater scale benefits that enable and incentivize more investment into innovative technologies.

With a larger population, you have more consumers to potentially buy new products, more startups bringing new ideas and products to market, more competition from industry peers, greater production capacity, deeper and more diverse supply chains, and larger pools of specialized labor.

With a shrinking population, you get less and less of each of those, especially as more and more developed nations across the world see their populations peak.

It isn't that there is or will likely be a shortage of capital anytime soon. But consumption is the driver of innovation, and young, talented workers are the practitioners of it. With less of those two things, we are likely to see less innovation and investment, not more.

On top of that, aging countries invariably spend more on the old than the young, which means that aging societies' resources will be increasingly allocated to old-age benefits and care rather than investment into future generations.

Macroeconomic Impact of Peak Humans

It is commonly thought that, all else equal, a shrinking working-age population would lead to widespread labor shortages, causing wage growth, inflation, and long-term interest rates to rise. But this line of thinking basically assumes consumer demand stays constant, which of course it wouldn't in a depopulation scenario.

People are consumers as well as workers. And older people, which shrinking populations have in abundance, consume less of most things (save healthcare and a few other items) than younger cohorts.

I am not aware of any country that has experienced an increase in inflation and interest rates as a result of population decline.

About a decade ago, the term "Japanification" sprang into existence to describe the economic impact of Japan's shrinking population.

Japan's GDP growth and inflation slowed to almost zero on average, which gradually pulled long-term interest down all the way to zero by the mid-2010s.

Of course, there have been a few brief and shallow spikes of inflation over the last three decades, but these have always been followed by a return to flat or even mildly deflationary conditions.

The current inflationary episode (if you can call 3% inflation an "episode") appears to be just a more prolonged case of temporary price growth, although it has lasted long enough to pull long-term interest rates up from zero.

With real consumption stagnant or gradually declining, it strikes me as highly unlikely that Japanese inflation will remain elevated for a sustained period of time.

China, whose population peaked in 2021, has experienced a very similar macroeconomic pattern as Japan.

Inflation was already quite tame as the Chinese population approached its peak, and now, after peaking, Chinese inflation has sunk to zero, pulling long-term interest rates down with it.

Of course, a declining population has not been China's only economic problem in recent years, but it is worth noting that the selective labor shortages China has experienced recently have not caused a general increase in inflation. Just the opposite.

Finally, it's worth noting that, based on my preliminary research, the European countries like Italy, Portugal, and Greece that are already shrinking experienced similar patterns of low and/or falling inflation and long-term interest rates -- at least until Russia invaded Ukraine and the European energy market was thrown into chaos.

"Japanification" appears to have added to its ranks "Chinafication" and "Eurofication."

As the United States gets closer and closer to peak population, whether that's in the 2030s or the 2050s, I would expect that, all else being equal, the US will look more and more like Japan, China, and Europe. That is, the average rate of inflation will decline, and so will GDP growth and long-term interest rates.

If you think that interest rates can't come down because the US government is spending money like a drunken sailor, take a look at the government debt-to-GDP ratios of Japan and China.

Older populations tend to save more, which creates marginal domestic buyers for their country's government bonds.

My DGI Buy List

Knowing how to invest in light of this information is tricky.

One line of reasoning that I find compelling goes that in a world of low and slowing GDP growth, the number of companies still capable of generating strong growth rates become more scarce and attract more investor dollars. And thus, in low-growth environments, growth stocks paradoxically command higher valuation multiples.

That narrative offers a convenient explanation for why growth stocks, especially those related to AI, have soared this year even as GDP growth has slowed.

The Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT), along with the broader technology group in the Nasdaq index (QQQ), have significantly outperformed the average S&P 500 stock (RSP).

As long as the economy isn't going into recession (as was feared in early April), low GDP growth paradoxically seems to be spurring more and more capital to flow into the relatively few stocks that can generate significant growth.

But there are other theories of what stocks might do in an environment of sustained (perhaps permanent) low growth and low inflation.

For example, in 2019 and then again later in 2021, we saw what some deemed the "everything bubble" in which yield chasing behavior drove strong performance in typically value-oriented dividend stocks as well as the exciting growth stocks.

In this environment, both the quality dividend growers in the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and the real estate companies in the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) performed almost as well as the S&P 500 (SPY) on a total return basis.

In 2018-2019, in fact, REITs outperformed both SPY and SCHD.

During that period, unlike 2021, when post-pandemic reopening was reviving economic growth, inflation, GDP growth, and interest rates were all low.

If the US economy does enter a prolonged state of low growth, low inflation, and low interest rates as a result of the slowing population growth discussed above, I believe my REIT-heavy and income-focused portfolio should shine, just as VNQ shined in 2019.

On that note, here's my buy list this week:

Once again this week, my plan is to prioritize buying at least a few shares each of the securities on my buy list, and if I have surplus cash, it will go into my current favorite cash equivalent ETF -- ICSH.

I'll just give some comments about a few of the picks.

Agree Realty

The mediocre stock price performance of ADC has been interesting lately. I would think that ADC would be a prime candidate to rally amid soft economic growth and the promise of Fed rate cuts to come soon. The REIT owns high-quality, single-tenant, net lease retail properties with contractual rent streams in place for about 10 years on average. Lower interest rates should be a huge boon to this constantly capital-raising REIT.

CEO Joey Agree was actively buying shares on the open market in the low $72 area. I have a personal rule that when Joey is buying, I buy too. I've never regretted it.

American Tower

AMT is another high-quality, defensive REIT that is inexplicably (to me) exhibiting weak share price performance right now.

Given its expected rebound in organic growth in the tower segment next year and beyond, and also considering the fact that subsidiary CoreSite's data centers are performing extraordinarily well, I don't really see a reason for pessimism. I'm happy to accumulate more shares.

H2O America

I love HTO's push to grow its footprint in my home state of Texas. The state's population continues to grow from domestic in-migration as well as, until recently, international immigration.

I also think that water utilities are a sneaky second-order beneficiary of AI and data centers, similar to electric utilities. Data centers require a lot of water for cooling purposes, and water utilities like HTO enjoy the volumetric growth from increased water usage and recycling.

data.title: How To Invest $100,000 In A Near-Perfect Strategy Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction:

When to invest and when not to invest?

There is never a clear answer to such questions, as we cannot predict the future. The legendary investor Warren Buffett has famously said, "Be fearful when others are greedy and greedy when others are fearful."

Well, that is all good, but most common retail investors are not adept at identifying when there is too much greed or fear in the market. In fact, if anything, retail investors are known to buy near the top and sell at the bottom. That's why it is necessary to focus on well-planned and time-tested strategies rather than on when to sell or buy. It is well proven that timing the market does not work for most folks.

After the tech crash of 2022, the market has done extremely well in the last two and a half years. The winning streak has continued in 2025 after a brief hiccup on the trade tensions. Also, due to the AI revolution, a lot of the gains have concentrated in the technology companies, especially the Mag-7. The net result is that the market is not cheap anymore. Almost everything seems to be overvalued.

So, our answer to the original question is that every time is a good time to invest, as long as it is with a solid and well-thought-out plan. Also, it may be wise to invest on a regular periodic basis rather than a lump sum.

What is a Near-Perfect Strategy (NPS) Portfolio?

We introduced this strategy back in 2019. You see, nothing is perfect in life, but if we can achieve 90% (or even 80%) of our goals, we would probably be happy with the results. That should be the mindset while designing a Near-Perfect Investment Strategy. This is akin to buying a house to live in for the long term. When we want to buy a house to live in, we are not looking to sell it the next year. Moreover, we want to make sure that the house has a solid foundation and structure, even if it requires some cosmetic upgrades. We also want to make sure that it is in a safe neighborhood with good schools and other common amenities. Also, it offers good value for the money being invested (not overpriced). Once we have purchased the house, we are not interested in monitoring its day-to-day market value, even though we want it to appreciate in the long term. If the housing market crashes, you are not looking to bail out of the house. You are still saving on rent and enjoying all the perks of the house, as you were prior to the crash. So, you don't bother with its price.

Similarly, a good investment portfolio is one that is likely to meet most of our stated goals. Once invested, you enjoy the income that it provides, but you are not looking at making a fast buck if something appreciates the next month, nor are you going to bail out of it if the market were to crash next month. That is the long-term investment mindset. Obviously, if the portfolio provides significant income, it certainly helps to ride out the volatility.

Goals and Objectives:

The following would be our goals for a Near-Perfect Strategy portfolio:-

  • The portfolio should provide roughly 5% to 6% income that is consistent and reliable, both in good years and bad.
  • The portfolio should at least match, preferably exceed, the growth of the S&P500. In terms of the long term, it should translate to at least a 10% annualized rate in terms of total returns.
  • To add the SWAN (sleep well at night) type attributes, the portfolio should aim to provide low volatility and lower drawdowns than the broader market.

Who Should Invest in the NPS Portfolio?

This portfolio should suit anyone who aspires to achieve financial freedom, whether they are younger folks in their 40s or someone who is about to retire. However, it may not suit folks who are very young and still in the early stages of their accumulation phase. In fact, we can categorize the potential investors (for the NPS portfolio) into three categories.

  1. Retirees and near-term retirees.
  2. Anyone above the age of 50 who wants to transition from a growth-oriented or index-based portfolio to an income and growth portfolio, but with less volatility and less stress.
  3. Anyone who aspires to achieve financial freedom early in life.

The Near-Perfect Strategy Portfolio:

The goals defined in the previous section are quite ambitious, as this aims to be a near-perfect portfolio. Nonetheless, they are not very hard to achieve with proper strategic planning and standing firm with the strategy in the good times and bad. This requires a strong level of conviction with the chosen strategy. If you do not have a strong conviction, it is best not to start such a strategy. Sure, one size does not fit all, and what may be appropriate for one person may not be so for another. So, it is always best to do your further research and due diligence until you are convinced of the strategy.

This portfolio will have three buckets.

  • DGI bucket with likely 4% Income
  • High-Income Funds bucket with 8% Income
  • Hedging bucket with 6% income.

(I) The DGI Bucket:

We consider the DGI (Dividend Growth Investing) bucket to serve the role of the foundational bucket in the overall portfolio. However, the income targets are moderate at best for this bucket, as many of the quality stocks never have a very high yield, even though they may be growing their dividends regularly. As their dividends grow, so does their price, and the yield never becomes too high. With your individual income targets, you could make the allocation to this bucket from 35% to 50% (or even more). For this bucket, we have two options:

  • Active DGI bucket, holding mostly individual company stocks. A group of 15-20 stocks should provide much-needed diversification. If you have too many of the stocks, the portfolio becomes difficult to manage and monitor all the individual stocks on a regular basis.
  • Passive DGI bucket, holding mostly dividend ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds). A carefully selected group of 5-6 funds should provide all the diversification you need and should serve most of the goals of this bucket. This one needs almost no monitoring, as first you are already paying the fund for the management, but also because no single position is large enough to dent your portfolio in any serious manner. Sure, some monitoring of each fund may be necessary at least on a yearly or semi-annual basis to make sure that the fund's management is not deviating from its stated goals.

In this article, we will include the 'Passive ETF-based DGI' bucket. It will consist of 5 ETFs with equal allocation.

(SCHD), (VYM), (VIG), (FDVV), and (JEPQ).

Table-1: DGI Bucket

SCHD is one of the popular dividend ETFs with nearly 100 large-cap dividend stocks, which provides a moderate level of yield. On the other hand, VYM is a low-cost dividend ETF from Vanguard with a large number of holdings, but with a lower yield than SCHD. We also include VIG, which is another low-cost ETF from Vanguard; however, the difference is that VIG is growth-oriented rather than high-yield. VIG tends to include companies that are likely to have higher dividend growth than the current yield. We include another ETF, namely FDVV from the Fidelity group, which includes both high-yield and high-growth types of stocks. Finally, we complete the bucket with JEPQ, an option-based income-focused ETF from the JPMorgan group. JEPQ will provide very high income and bring the overall income for the bucket to a very respectable 4% plus.

As a result, the overall bucket will be highly balanced and diversified. Some of the largest positions will be: Broadcom (AVGO), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Exxon (XOM), Home Depot (HD), AbbVie (ABBV), Chevron (CVX), and Amazon (AMZN). However, the top 10 holdings would likely account for only about 20%.

(II) The High-Income Bucket:

We will give the least allocation to this bucket, maybe 25% plus or minus. The purpose of this bucket will be two-fold. First, to provide a high level of income (between 8% to 9%) so as to compensate for the relatively lower yield from the DGI bucket. The second purpose is to provide some exposure to alternative types of asset classes.

We will select a combination of CEFs (Closed-End Funds) and some BDCs (Business Development Cos) for this bucket. These two types of instruments generally provide a high level of income. In fact, there is a third category, REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts), which can also provide high income. However, we will include a couple of CEFs to represent diversified exposure to REITs. CEFs can generally trade at discounts or premiums to their NAVs (Net asset values), so we should pay attention to what we are paying. All things equal, we should buy them when they are offering a higher discount than their long-term averages. That said, since we are building the portfolio for the long term, we should buy only quality funds with a solid history of performance.

We select the following 6 CEFs from different asset classes, each one with a solid track record. We also selected BIZD, an ETF fund of BDCs.

(ADX), (EOS), (PDI), (RLTY), (BME), (UTF), and (BIZD).

Table-2: High-Income Bucket

Note: In the above table, PDI is trading at a significant premium; it may be best to accumulate this in a few installments rather than a lump sum.

(III) The Hedging Bucket:

This is the only bucket in this portfolio that is not a buy-and-hold type. Instead, it will require monthly trading, but mostly one or two trades a month. For this reason, it will be much more preferable to have this bucket inside a tax-deferred type of account. That being said, most likely, it will require less than an hour's worth of work (likely much less) on a monthly basis.

We will use four securities for regular use, plus one security as the hedging security.

Table-3: List of 5 securities:

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of QQQ after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside a rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only when it is performing well. More conservative investors could replace it with QQQ.

The process to determine the monthly rotation:

Step-1: At the end of each month, we will calculate the performance (total returns, including any dividends) of all five securities over the last 3 and 7 months. We will calculate the average performance based on 3-month and 7-month periods.

Step-2: We will see if any of the regular four securities (ADX, RQI, KYN, QLD) have performed worse than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then we will replace that security with IEF for the next month.

Step-3: We will keep the investment from Step-2 for the entire month.

Step-4: At the end of the month, we will repeat the process (from Step-1 through 3).

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

In addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth, this rotation strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (our rough estimate at 6%). Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8%), while the fourth one, QLD, provides almost no income. Also, due to rotation, at times, the hedging security with a lower yield may replace the regular security with a higher yield. So, on average, we expect the portfolio income to be roughly 6%.
