Risks and potential concerns:

There are several risks that an investor should be aware of prior to investing in the strategy covered above.

  • Market risk: Barring a couple of investments, nearly all of the investments in this portfolio are invested in equity stocks. So, if the broader market were to correct or take a deep dive for any unforeseen reason, this portfolio would also lose value, though likely to a lesser extent due to the hedging portfolio.
  • Limited downside protection: Only one of the three buckets (which only represents 1/3rd of the capital) has an in-built hedging mechanism. The other two buckets, representing 2/3rd of the capital, have no downside protection or hedging. In case of a deep correction, especially the CEF income bucket, it will likely lose value as much as the S&P500, sometimes more.
  • Tax implications from Bucket-3: The hedging bucket requires monthly trading (from 0 to 4 trades). If this bucket were implemented inside a taxable account, the investor would have to track the gains and losses, and there may be significant short-term tax gains and tax liability. However, there would be no such issues if this were implemented inside a tax-deferred account.
  • Ongoing Rotation effort: There is going to be an ongoing monthly effort to maintain rotation on bucket-3. This requires discipline and a certain level of conviction to stay on the course even during times when this bucket may be underperforming.

Concluding Thoughts:

The market has gained significantly in the last two and a half years and appears expensive. More recently, the market has surged forward on the renewed hopes of the Fed's dovish turn. That being said, we can't be certain of the future direction of the market. That's why we need to focus on what we invest in rather than when to invest. We have presented an investment strategy with three diverse buckets, each serving a specific set of goals. Together, they will provide roughly 6% income and market-matching (likely market-exceeding) returns in the long term, all the while keeping drawdowns low.

data.title: 4 Big Reasons Why American Markets Continue To Rise
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the broader investment backdrop, with a specific focus on American equities. In this review I will take a look at how US stocks have been rising - and keep on rising - despite a number of macro-risks that have the potential to derail the party. This is timely because 2025 has been a banner year for the major US indices even with the April draw-downs, as illustrated below:

As you can see, assets are up across the board this year. The three largest indexes are posting healthy gains and even defensive metals, like gold, and risk-on alternative assets, like Bitcoin, are soaring.

This begs the question: how or why is this happening?

Of course there are a number of factors why stocks (or any other asset) rise and fall. But in this exercise I will touch on some of the primary reasons why I see these gains are justified and why they could potentially even continue in the weeks and months ahead. Understanding the investment backdrop is critical to forming a coherent thesis, and I believe the attributes I will cover here will help readers make informed decisions going forward.

Earnings Have Broadened Out

One of the primary reasons for the continued rise in stocks has been the broadening out of earnings. This has led to investor confidence that markets aren't in a "bubble" or just the Mag 7 dominating sentiment (perhaps irrationally). Rather, earnings growth has actually declined for the Mag 7 while increasing for other baskets (notably the rest of the S&P 500):

Of course it isn't great to see any company, sector, or basket decline in their earnings growth. But the positive is that this isn't happening across the board. Other companies within the S&P 500 and Russell 2000 are seeing an uptick in earnings growth, with the expectation that is going to continue into 2026.

If that does indeed materialize, it reduces the pressure on the Mag 7 to continually beat expectations and drive markets higher. This makes for a healthier market overall - in my view - and will also be important for sustaining the new highs the indices are setting right now. The takeaway for me is that this helps improve confidence in the broader market - not just the past or current leaders - and will limit the amount of panic selling or profit taking in the short term, sustaining recent gains.

History Keeps Investors Invested

A second attribute is one that has stood the test of time since I started my investment journey. And it is that, over time, those who remain in the market outperform those who don't. So this trait isn't necessarily isolated to just the most recent market rally, but it is inclusive of why staying the course wins out over most cycles.

The simple fact is that equities win in the long run. That is why people stay invested - or even continue to pile more cash into equities - when indices hit record levels. You would think that logically people would become risk averse as markets climb higher but many are simply agnostic to the current price. They continue to plow money into their 401k or pension accounts and even continue to buy up shares on the open market. The rationale is that equities have a long track record of beating cash and beating inflation when we stretch time periods out:

As the graphic above shows us, equities have a very high likelihood of beating inflation in almost all scenarios. When inflation is high (above 3%) and getting higher, that is where the more challenging environment comes in. But even then, there is a strong case for staying invested given that almost half the time equities are going to still beat out inflation. But in the other environments, it is an extremely likely scenario for equities to post strong returns. This type of historic precedence keeps people (including myself) invested in equity markets even when frothiness becomes a concern.

We can expand this type of methodology even to more limited baskets, such as "growth stocks" or the Mag 7 (which is dominating growth trackers at the moment). Some may be thinking that it is time to focus on unloved or undervalued sectors as Growth/Tech continue to lead the way. Makes sense, right? It sure does have logic to it. Yet fresh cash continues to pile into the NASDAQ 100 and other growth-oriented funds that are dominated by the highest of fliers in the Mag 7. Why would that be the case?

The answer is similar to the above that we often see a self-fulfilling prophecy when it comes to investing. Growth has often won in the past so investors want to bank on it winning again in the future. Despite growth's historic run and the potential for it being overvalued here, investors don't want to miss out that growth will keep on winning. So they buy it up and growth continues to win. This has been a common trend over the past five, ten, and twenty year time spans and it very well could keep on continuing:

Now I am not saying that this means that growth is going to win again with certainty. Nor am I saying this is the time to "load up" on this thematic idea. What I am trying to do is uncover why some ideas or sectors keep on winning when metrics like valuation or volatility suggest a reversal of fortune is due.

Winners can stay winners for a long time, whether because of momentum, historical returns, earnings, or a combination of those and other factors. When investors see stats above that show growth winning a large majority of the time, they may want to try to ride that wave rather than bet on its backpedaling. This can help drive an idea or theme higher - as we are seeing right now - to the dismay of value-oriented investors.

Inflation Has Settled Into Lower Range

A third trait behind the market's rise in 2025 has been that inflation has come off its highs from 2022 and 2023. While still elevated compared to the Fed's prior target of 2%, investors have come to terms with inflation that it is here - and here to stay. The positive takeaway from this backdrop is that while inflation is still present it has settled into a noticeably lower range than where it has been:

This has helped encourage investors to stay in the market because the more dire situation of high—and - and increasing - inflation has moderated. It has entered into a more reasonable range and provided us with the potential conclusion that the "worst has passed".

The outcome on the inflation front has likely taken some onlookers by surprise given the emergence of tariffs from the Trump administration. This had many forecasters and media pundits proclaim that inflation would spike higher as a result and consumers were going to face much higher prices. Through August at least, this has proven not to be the case, helping support higher equity prices.

One would probably wonder how this was possible since so many were predicting much higher prices as a result of the tariffs. While the future remains to be seen, the positive news for US consumers is that prices have not soared higher because companies are eating a larger share of the tariff than expected, rather than passing on the full cost of them as many predicted (or feared).

Take Canada as an example. A vast majority of exporters are absorbing costs of tariffs and reducing their margins. According to a recent survey, a self-reported group of almost 70% of business owners/managers advised they are responding to higher tariffs with a reduction in their own margins when asked:

Now, this can present a different set of problems longer term. If companies begin to shrink margins to accommodate new taxes (tariffs) then share prices could see some pressure. So too could earnings multiples, which have been a big driver of gains in the latest bull market.

But the focus of this review is why American stocks are doing so well. Part of the reason is that the tariff headwind for US consumers has not materialized into a meaningful impact just yet. Further, foreign companies are choosing to reduce their own margins rather than relying on the US importers to do so, which would hurt US share prices disproportionately. Thus far, tariffs have not been the big thorn many feared they would, as the April sell-off projected. I am not personally banking on this lasting forever but it does help explain why US stocks have been having the run they have despite this risk.

American Investors Benefit From the USD

The final thought here is how Americans - and American shareholders of US firms - continue to benefit from the reality of the US dollar (USD) as the global currency. I would emphasize that coming out of the early Q2 fallout for stocks, both domestic and non-US investors have been piling back into US stocks. The net flows for US-based investors into US ETFs have been rising consistently and July also save a net positive move from overseas as well:

Part of this has to do with the other three features I talked about here. But the fourth is how the US currency continues to dominate global trade and exchange reserves. This keeps demand for US dollars and assets elevated, all other things being equal, and helps instill a sense of confidence in US financial markets that is difficult for other countries to match. This means that despite fleeting trade concerns, geopolitical risks, and valuation concerns, the US has this inherent benefit that keeps investors interested:

The conclusion to draw here is that the USD's dominance is clear, quite large, and not going anywhere any time soon. There just simply isn't a viable alternative (yet) given how the Euro, British Pound, and Japanese Yen all have problems of their own that limit their replacement of the USD to date. While this could change in the future, it helps to explain the continuing resilience of US markets in the face of adversity.

Bottom-Line

US markets keep on rising and that certainly poses a concern for those interested in "value". But it is important to remember that markets can rise for a long time and bull markets can be painful to miss. In this review I touched on some reasons why US indices have had the run they have and how that may continue. While I certainly wouldn't get too "risk-on" at this juncture, trying to time the market in the event there "might be" a reversal can often be a fool's game. Hopefully this piece gives investors some clarity on why equities continue to run higher and also offers some confidence that staying the course can be rewarding.

data.title: Capturing AI's Upside: ARTY's Concentrated Play On Tomorrow's Winners
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a world where AI ceases to be a promise and becomes a real engine of disruption, iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY) seeks to capture the exponential growth of this phenomenon in a pure way.

The fund is not limited to surfing the traditional technological wave; its bet is to position itself in the center of the AI universe, covering both those who develop the most advanced algorithms and companies that provide infrastructure, chips, software, and services that make possible the massive expansion of artificial intelligence.

It is not a diversified ETF to use; here risk and opportunity are enhanced. ARTY concentrates more than 83% of its portfolio in technology, with selective bets on names such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Vertiv (VRT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO) and Arista Networks (ANET). If you are looking for specific, global, and direct exposure to the AI boom, this fund offers it with conviction.

But the appeal of ARTY goes beyond their names. The fund captures the entire AI value chain, from chip design to cloud services and critical infrastructure for data centers and companies that bring artificial intelligence to the end user. Expected growth in the sector is extraordinary: according to Gartner, more than 80% of companies will adopt generative AI applications by 2026 (vs. less than 5% in 2023), and Goldman Sachs' estimates suggest that the impact on global GDP could exceed $7T by 2030.

In this scenario, the thesis is clear: ARTY is a focused, high-risk, and high-potential move to capture the next stage of the tech rally-especially if artificial intelligence continues to expand its real applications and market weight.

Background and strategy overview

ARTY relaunches in August 2024 under its new strategy, focusing on the Morningstar Global Artificial Intelligence Select Index. This implies a debugging of its universe: only companies with a relevant role in AI enter, be it in infrastructure, software, data, services, or the development of generative applications.

The ETF holds 49 positions, which makes it highly concentrated. Its top ten holdings account for more than 41% of the fund. The sectoral composition is overwhelmingly technological, with marginal exposure to communications, industrials, and utilities. There are no banks, energy, or real estate; here everything revolves around AI, data centers, semiconductors, and digital service companies.

Among the most important positions are AMD (6.0%), Super Micro Computer (5.7%), Vertiv Holdings (5.2%), NVIDIA (5.1%), and Arista Networks (4.9%), all companies linked to the heart of AI infrastructure and hardware. This selection is relevant: the demand for GPUs, data center infrastructure, and cloud services is the real "peak and blade" of AI.

The fund also exhibits some global bias: in addition to US companies, it includes positions in Japan (Advantest), Europe, and other developed markets, although most of the portfolio is US-centric.

Performance, valuation, and key metrics

Since its benchmark change, ARTY has achieved an outstanding performance: last year, the price rose by +30.8% and total profitability reached +31%, doubling the S&P 500 in the same period (+14.5% and +16%, respectively). At 3 years, the fund accumulates +52.6% vs. +58.5% of the S&P 500 Total Return, and in 5 years, although its performance is lower (+40.8% vs. +105.6% of the S&P), the spread is partly explained by the timing of the change in the index and the high volatility of the tech sector.

Volatility is remarkable: 3-year standard deviation of 25.1% and beta of 1.31 against the S&P 500. It is a fund for those who tolerate large movements, as shown also by the high tracking error and the annual turnover of 119%.

Portfolio valuation reflects appetite for growth: average P/E is 37.9x and P/B is 6.35x, well above the general market. Here you pay for the future, not for the present. The cost ratio (expense ratio) is 0.47%, aligned with other BlackRock-themed ETFs, and very reasonable for the niche.

The dividend is almost symbolic (0.16% per annum), and distribution policy is secondary to potential growth. It is not an ETF to seek yield; reinvestment and capital appreciation are the absolute focus.

Momentum metrics reinforce the recent appeal: in all 1M, 6M, and 1Y terms, ARTY outperforms ETFs by a wide margin, achieving an A+ rating on Momentum. However, in terms of 5 years, the rating is low (C), showing the volatility of the sector and the change of winners over time.

Growth, risks, and portfolio considerations

The projected growth of the sector is extraordinary. Data center spending, semiconductor demand, and advances in AI (generative AI, machine learning, cloud services) applications are accelerating year by year. Not surprisingly, major fund positions are tied to "picks and shovels" providers for modern digital mining.

But the cost of this bet is volatility. ARTY is not diversified by sector; more than 83% is pure technology, and its main holdings may experience sharp declines in the face of any profit-taking or correction in the sector. Regulatory risk on AI, fierce competition, and possible technological obsolescence are real threats. In addition, geographic concentration in the US, despite some global diversification, adds a bias to specific regulatory or fiscal shocks.

Liquidity is good (AUM of $1.26B, high daily volume), and the cost structure is competitive. However, tracking error, rotation, and volatility are high, which can make it difficult to actively manage tactical positions.

Conclusion: Who is ARTY for?

ARTY is an ideal instrument for investors with high conviction in the future of artificial intelligence and volatility tolerance, who seek to capture the upside of a mega-trend even far from their roof. It is not a central position for all portfolios; its concentration and its tech bias make it suitable as a "satellite" or complement of a diversified strategy.

The expectation is clear: if AI continues to expand and industry "picks & shovels" maintain their traction, ARTY can continue delivering higher returns. But investors must be prepared to navigate storms: when technology corrects, it does so without anesthesia.

In summary, I consider that ARTY summarizes the bet on the digital future, with all its risks and opportunities. A high-risk move, high potential and a strong growth narrative, ideal for those who believe that the upcoming bull market will continue to speak in algorithmic language.

data.title: Tilray Brands: Momentum Now, Fundamentals Later
data.description:
Tilray Brands, Inc. (TLRY) has recently emerged on my radar after the recent increase in volume following a policy-fueled rally. I argue that a sell-on-the-news drop is brewing once the Schedule III decision is finally announced (either positive or negative). Until then, shares could tick up as more speculators pile into the stock.

I see the share price at a critical resistance level in the chart. If it breaks the $1.5 level, I may open a small speculative position in the stock and try to ride the momentum wave for a quick double digit gain. However, at the moment of writing the article, I don't have enough conviction that the momentum wave will sustain. Why?

Well, the pending Schedule III decision could remove the 280E burden. However, Tilray’s core cannabis is in Canada and EMEA, so the company won't benefit from this tailwind since its U.S. footprint is mostly beer and beverage. Growth has slowed since November 2023, and the non-GAAP P/E is 34, about 89% above the sector median. I don't think the premium is justified once the Schedule III hype fades away.

Back to my point, I will wait for a clean break above $1.50 before a speculative long. I am happy to stay on the sidelines in the meantime.

The Policy Narrative

Notably, the single most important factor that drove the recent rally was Trump's remarks on Aug 11, saying that he’ll decide on the reclassification of cannabis “in the coming weeks.”

To better understand why this is relevant, I considered including below a quick recap of the events that led to Trump's remarks. On October 6, 2022, President Biden directed the HHS and DEA to review marijuana’s schedule. In August 2023, the HHS recommended Schedule III after an FDA 8-factor analysis finding currently accepted medical use of the drug. In May 2024, the DOJ announces and publishes the NPRM to transfer marijuana to Schedule III. In August of the same year, the DEA announces it will hold a hearing before an ALJ on the proposal. This was scheduled for January this year; however, the DEA postponed the hearing "pending resolution of an appeal filed by a party in the proceedings." If this wasn't enough, in July, the presiding ALJ (John J. Mulrooney II) retired and stated that all matters are forwarded to the new DEA Administrator. Terry Cole was confirmed by the Senate as DEA Administrator in late July 2025, and as of today, the process remains paused (whether to resume the hearing is at the Administrator’s discretion).

As you can see, the efforts to move cannabis to Schedule III trace back almost three years. Therefore, investors welcomed Trump's decision in the coming weeks, as it will bring certainty from a regulatory and tax perspective.

To be specific, Schedule III will remove the 280E tax penalty for U.S. operators. This means that US cannabis companies will be able to deduct normal business expenses, improving their bottom lines. Even though this is not a full legalization, Schedule III is widely expected to improve access to banking and lower capital costs.

The big question is how Tilray will benefit from these tailwinds. Well, I see no direct 280E benefit to Tilray as its core cannabis operations are in Canada and EMEA. In the US, the footprint of this conglomerate is mainly related to beer and beverage alcohol products.

In my view, the upside may come from an M&A perspective. If U.S. cannabis companies experience an improvement in cash flows due to the 280E relief, sector valuations could lift and dealmaking opens up. As you may know, Tilray is a massive conglomerate company, so acquisitions in the US could be the way forward after the Schedule III move.

More Bullish Signals

Insider buying looks promising, particularly by the CEO and CFO. As seen below, the CEO bought $100k worth of shares on July 30, at an average price of $0.61. Not a bad trade, considering today's share price.

Aside from the favorable insider buying activity, volume trends look very favorable for a momentum play. The table below shows the metrics of my screener, with a 5-day average volume 58% above the 3-month average.

The recent increase in volume is supported by a sell side boost from Jefferies, reiterating a buy rating and raising its TLRY price target to $2. Furthermore, the company regained the Nasdaq minimum bid compliance after 10 consecutive days above the minimum threshold of $1.

What's Next

If the share price breaks beyond $1.5, I believe it is likely we could see a continuation of the bull run.

However, I am not buying today. I prefer to wait for a breakout. Why?

Take a look at the revenue growth rate over the past few years.

The decline in growth since November 2023 is significant. Furthermore, the company is trading at a 1-year non-GAAP P/E ratio of 34. To put that figure into perspective, that's 89% above the sector median. Considering the slow growth, I don't believe the premium is justified. Therefore, the bull case revolves around the company being able to sustain its volume. As soon as volume goes down, I would expect the share price to follow. It is likely that will happen after the decision for Schedule III has been officially made (i.e., a sell on the news). Until then, I prefer to see the share price. If it breaks above the $1.5 level, I may consider opening a speculative long position in this stock.

data.title: Micron: Time To Jump Back On The AI Bullet Train (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Micron (NASDAQ:MU) seems exceptionally well-positioned to capitalize on a few key structural tailwinds I've been tracking in the memory market. We’re seeing DRAM experience a pretty sharp pricing trend going into 2H25. In my previous coverage, I downgraded Micron to a strong sell, against the run of the tide. My take was that the positives were already priced in after 3Q25 earnings, and indeed the stock did drop 14% after that sell before recovering. My mistake is that I missed the window to upgrade at the bottom, and as a result, this update is coming pretty late. However, there is no denying that Micron is now back onto that upward trend supported by AI, with DRAM prices making a strong recovery, as I'll explain. Additionally, the company’s recent guidance hike for 4QFY25, which raises revenue to $11.2 billion, gross margins to 44.5%, and EPS to $2.85, signals that DRAM continues to fuel growth. At the same time, Micron’s HBM capabilities position it at the heart of this AI infrastructure boom we’re going through. If we take these factors together, they now make Micron a compelling stock to own with strong near- and long-term upside. The stock is currently trading in the $117 range, down about 8% from the $127 highs we saw prior to the 3Q earnings. Now seems like an opportunity to get back into Micron at a discount, in my opinion.

DRAM price spike

If we take a look at Micron’s latest guidance hike, it really tells us just how powerful the DRAM pricing environment has become. Management, of course, lifted that 4Q25 revenue expectation to $11.2 billion from $10.7 billion. Now, this may seem like a small shift, but consider it in the context of its gross margin targets being raised to 44.5% from 42%. On top of that, we saw EPS estimates jump. This isn’t a minor adjustment; we’re seeing a significant upward revision, which indicates the firm has considerable confidence in future pricing. So, what's behind this pricing push? I'd have to say the stronger outlook is sustained DRAM momentum. Unlike the volatile cycles we saw in the past, this strength is being supported by broad-based demand across AI infrastructure. We’re seeing a huge spike in data center demand, especially for AI use, going into 2H25. Mobile markets are also seeing a push in the right direction. What’s particularly notable here is how pricing has held up even against elevated supply expectations.

Going into Micron's recent earnings report, I expected that DRAM prices would fall through for the current quarter. But, DRAM average selling prices have been surprisingly resilient, and with AI-related demand pushing memory intensity higher, we are finally seeing Micron leverage its scale and cost structure to expand those margins. The revised gross margin outlook of 44.5% is a level not seen in years, and it suggests Micron is now in a margin expansion cycle rather than simply benefiting from temporary demand spikes that we used to wait for. I've been tracking DRAM prices for quite some time now. I’ve predicted the price changes in the sector, shown in the table below.

In my opinion, Micron’s pricing power is no longer just cyclical; it’s gaining a solid growth platform by demand growth from AI and next-gen data workloads.

HBM & AI Infrastructure

We have to mention Micron’s growing dominance in High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). Right now, it's positioned nicely at the center of this AI infrastructure boom. It's another structural tailwind for the company. HBM, of course, is a critical enabler of next-generation AI systems. Massive throughput and low latency are required to train and run increasingly complex models. Micron is actually one of only a handful of suppliers capable of producing advanced HBM. It has recent design wins like its qualification for Nvidia’s AI accelerators. Many investors are starting to tip HBM as a catalyst for Micron. HBM is at the core of any AI infrastructure. It serves as the memory equivalent of GPUs and custom accelerators, which require extremely fast and efficient memory to handle massive training datasets. We’re seeing that demand for HBM is accelerating at a pace far greater than traditional DRAM. Hyperscalers and chipmakers are rushing to secure supply amid surging AI investment. And it seems Micron has already sold out its HBM capacity for 2025, a clear indication of demand health. When it comes to HBM, unlike commodity DRAM, where pricing can fluctuate with consumer cycles, HBM carries much better pricing and higher margins. As AI models grow larger and more complex, the memory requirements per server will continue expanding exponentially. The HBM demand dynamic is set to act as a sustained catalyst for Micron’s revenue and margin expansion well beyond the current cycle. For example, since 2010, the number of parameters in AI models has approximately doubled every year. For instance, OpenAI's GPT-4 boasted 1.8 trillion parameters, which was a significant leap from the previous models. To illustrate this growth, consider the chart below. It’s an exponential increase in AI model parameters, so there’s going to be a corresponding rise in memory requirements. And since HBM demand is expected to outpace the broader memory market, with pricing power firmly in Micron’s hands, I expected the company to deliver outsized growth and margin expansion on that front.

Valuation

Micron currently seems quite attractive compared to some of its peers in the information technology sector. On a forward-looking basis, the company has a price-to-sales ratio of 3.51, just slightly above its five-year average of 3.49 and about 10.8% higher than the sector median of 3.17. That premium suggests Micron is priced slightly above its peers on a sales basis, but it reflects market expectations for a slight rebound in profitability as those memory markets recover. Similarly, its forward EV to sales ratio sits at 3.62, about 13.2% above the sector’s 3.20 median. This is just marginally higher than its historical average of 3.52. Going a bit deeper, Micron’s looks even more compelling to me when factoring in profitability expectations. The company trades at a forward EV to EBITDA of 7.4, a very steep discount of over 50% versus the sector median of 14.8, and a forward price to earnings of just 14.4 compared to the sector’s 23.7. Micron’s earnings power appears significantly undervalued relative to its peer group, particularly given its forecasted EPS growth for 2025. Although Micron carries a slight revenue multiple premium, its earnings-based valuations are significantly cheaper than those of the sector. This makes Micron a strong relative value play. I think investors are getting both a cyclical recovery upside and also a discounted entry point compared to the broader technology universe.

What's next?

All in all, we’re seeing Micron enter 2H25 with much more structural demand from AI and data center workloads. It's likely to drive continued growth and margin expansion. Investors should monitor DRAM and HBM pricing trends closely, as I am. They can tell us how well Micron can sustain its momentum. With AI-related memory demand showing no signs of slowing and raised guidance, the company gets my upgrade to a buy.

data.title: SLYG: Skewed Towards Tariff Trouble
data.description:
The SPDR® S&P 600™ Small Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:SLYG) has been underperforming the general, larger cap skewed market (SPY) by a pretty significant degree over various time horizons, but also over the horizon since tariffs became a central economic and trading question.

While we continue to hunt for deals in international markets, there are some structural issues faced by a portfolio skewed towards the world of US smaller cap stocks, which are industrial and quite vulnerable to tariffs. We agree with the laggard price performance of SLYG compared to SPY and wouldn't bet necessarily on a convergence given also the macroeconomic risks.

SLYG Breakdown

SLYG is a passive index tracker with the following sectoral exposures. The expense ratio is pretty limited at 0.15%, which is about in line with lower cost passive ETFs and is pretty good considering the more narrow mandate inherent in a mid to small-cap ETF.

Beginning with the industrial exposures where tariffs would have the most impact, the exposure is 22.55%. Our concern is that the exposure to tariffs, particularly to China where it's looking like tariff are going to be highest, is greater among smaller industrial players. Larger companies are more capable of competing with manufacturing scale in the domestic market, or having more diversified supply chains, utilising other geographies like Mexico, Philippines, Japan or Vietnam. Small industrial companies are likely to have more concentrated supplier exposure and it's more likely to be an exposure to China as it's ultimately the workshop of the world. Larger companies typically have less China reliance than peers. In the latest GDP/GDI readings, corporate profits are up slightly up in Q2 as opposed to the Q1 of the year. The extent that this can continue will be dependent on the ability for corporates to pass on costs to consumers, which will depend on consumer capacity to absorb further price increases in the current macroeconomic environment. These smaller cap industrials are going to generally have lesser pricing power though a greater need for it due to their tendency to have greater tariff exposure to trade flows hit hardest by tariffs.

The other major exposure here is to financial services. The concern markets might have here is around the labour market situation. In the Jackson Hole speech by Powell, the Fed revealed that it is noticing still balanced S/D in labour markets, but only because of a possibly unsustainable decline in S amid a decline in D. At some point the labour market would become impacted by the declines in consumer sentiment and activity. Related to that, the GDP performance in Q2 was less to do with consumer activity and more to do with a rebound of export relative to import behaviour after the Q1 frontloading activity ahead of tariffs. Financial services, particularly regional banks that are focused on lending and savings activities, are likely to reserve more on their small business and mortgage portfolios should something as fundamental as the labour market take a turn for the worse.

Bottom Line

SPY also has a decent amount of financial and consumer discretionary exposure, but there is more of a service component in SPY that is less affected by tariffs. We mostly agree with the relative outperformance of SPY over SLYG, even if the fact that the general market is being driven by the AI trade is something that we don't view positively. Given the macro dynamics, from an ETF perspective we'd be more interested in low credit risk bets right now in longer duration fixed income. Otherwise, we continue to look at single stocks that aren't exposed to the general macroeconomic environment.
